content.problemStatement:
You are a premium member of Chef Airlines which means you can store milesmilesmiles in your Chef Airlines account. If you currently have xxx miles in your Chef Airlines account then you can travel xxx miles for free. You start from the airport labelled 000 and wish to reach airport labelled NNN. Following are the rules of travel :



If you are at the airport labelled iii, you can travel to any airport labelled jjj, only if j>ij > ij>i;




If you travel from airport labelled iii to airport labelled jjj, you end up exhausting j−ij - ij−i miles from your Chef Airlines account. Consequently, you can travel from airport labelled iii to airport labelled jjj if and only if j−i≤xj - i \leq xj−i≤x, where xxx is the number of miles in your Chef Airlines account when you departed from the airport labelled iii.




Whenever you arrive at airport labelled iii, you can add up to MiM_iMi​ number of miles in your Chef Airlines account. There is no limit to the number of miles your Chef Airlines account can hold.




You start by arriving at airport labelled 111 with 000 miles in your Chef Airlines account.



Determine the minimum number of airports you must visit in order to reach the airport labelled NNN. If it is not possible to do so, print −1-1−1.


Input Format

First line will contain TTT, number of testcases. Then the testcases follow.
The first line of each testcase contains NNN integers.
The second line of each testcase contains NNN integers : M1,M2...MNM_1, M_2 . . . M_NM1​,M2​...MN​.


Output Format
For each testcase, print the minimum number of airports you must visit in order to reach the airport labelled NNN. If it isn't possible to reach airport labelled NNN, print "-1" (without quotes).
content.inputFormat:
The first line contains an integer T, the number of test cases. For each test case:
- The first line contains an integer N, the number of airports (they are labelled from 1 to N).
- The second line contains N space‑separated integers M1, M2, …, MN where Mi is the maximum number of miles you may add to your account when you arrive at airport i.
content.outputFormat: For each test case output a single line containing the minimum number of airports you must visit (including the starting airport 1 and the destination airport N) to reach airport N. If it is impossible, output -1.

sampleTestCases.explanation:
Explanation:
Test-case 1:

You reach airport labelled 111 with 000 miles in your Chef Airlines account.
You add 333 miles to your Chef Airlines account. You now have 333 miles in your Chef Airlines account.
You reach airport labelled 333 with 111 miles remaining in your Chef Airlines account.
You add 222 miles to your Chef Airlines account. You now have 333 miles in your Chef Airlines account.
You reach airport labelled 555 with 111 mile remaining in your Chef Airlines account.
You add 444 miles to your Chef Airlines account. You now have a total of 555 miles in your Chef Airlines account which is enough to reach airport labelled N=10N = 10N=10.

You visited a total of 444 airports.

solution.approach:
The problem is identical to the classic "minimum number of refuelling stops" problem. Consider the distance from airport 1 to airport N as N‑1 units. You start with 0 miles of fuel. When you arrive at airport i you may add any amount up to Mi miles – taking the whole Mi is never worse than taking less, because extra miles can only help you later.

We process the airports from left to right, keeping a max‑heap of the Mi values of all airports we have already passed (including the current one). While we have no fuel left to move to the next airport, we "retroactively" decide to stop at the previously passed airport with the largest Mi, add its miles to our fuel, and increase the stop counter. If the heap is empty when we need fuel, the destination is unreachable.

When we finally reach airport N, the number of stops performed equals the number of intermediate airports where we added fuel (the start airport is counted if we used its miles). The required answer is stops + 1 (the destination airport).

The algorithm runs in O(N log N) per test case because each airport is inserted into the heap once and each stop removes the maximum element from the heap.
solution.timeComplexity: O(N log N) per test case, where N is the number of airports.
